options.explanation: These are the semilunar valves located at the exit of the heart. Their function is to ensure that blood flows into the arteries and does not leak back into the ventricles once they relax.

This indicates the thick muscular wall of the heart known as the myocardium. It is responsible for the powerful contraction that pushes blood out of the heart chambers and into the circulatory system.
These are the chordae tendineae, which are strong fibrous cords. They act like parachute strings to connect the heart valves to the muscles, preventing the valves from flapping back into the atria.

These are the papillary muscles, which are specialized muscular pillars inside the ventricles. Their function is to contract and pull the chordae tendineae tight to keep the heart valves closed during a heartbeat.

questions.explanation: The correct choice is D because option D points to the papillary muscles, which anchor the heart valves. While option C points to the strings and option A points to the valves themselves, the papillary muscles are the specific muscular parts that pull those strings to prevent the valves from turning inside out. Option B is simply the general heart wall, which does not have the specialized anchoring function of the papillary muscles.

questions.text: In the human heart, tricuspid valve closes when
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This scenario is related to the aortic valve and the left side of the heart, not the tricuspid valve on the right side.

options.text: Left atrial pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure
options.explanation: This is associated with the mitral (bicuspid) valve on the left side of the heart, not the tricuspid valve on the right side

options.text: Right ventricular pressure exceeds right atrial pressure
options.explanation: The tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and the right ventricle. Its function is to prevent the backflow of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium during the contraction of the ventricle. When the right ventricle contracts (ventricular systole), the pressure in the right ventricle increases. The tricuspid valve closes to prevent blood from flowing back into the relaxed right atrium. This closure ensures that blood is forced into the pulmonary artery and sent to the lungs for oxygenation.

options.text: Pulmonary trunk pressure exceeds ventricular pressure
options.explanation: This is related to the pulmonary valve and the right ventricular ejection of blood into the pulmonary artery, not the closure of the tricuspid valve.

questions.explanation: The Tricuspid valve closes when Right ventricular pressure exceeds right atrial pressure (C).The closure of the Tricuspid valve marks the very start of the ventricular systole (contraction). As the right ventricle begins to contract, the pressure inside the ventricle rises rapidly. When this rising pressure becomes greater than the pressure remaining in the right atrium, the three cusps of the Tricuspid valve are forced upward, snapping shut. This closure prevents the blood from being pushed back into the right atrium, ensuring that all the blood is instead forced forward into the pulmonary trunk. The closure of the tricuspid and bicuspid valves produces the first heart sound.

questions.text: A patient brought to a hospital with myocardial infarction is normally immediately given
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Penicillin is an antibiotic used to treat bacterial infections; it has no role in treating a myocardial infarction (heart attack).

This is correct. Streptokinase is a thrombolytic (clot-busting) drug that is often administered immediately to a patient suffering a heart attack to dissolve the blood clot that is blocking the coronary artery.
This is incorrect. Cyclosporin-A is an immunosuppressant drug used to prevent the rejection of transplanted organs; it is not used as an immediate treatment for a heart attack.

This is incorrect. Statins are long-term drugs used to lower blood cholesterol levels to prevent future heart attacks; they are not used as an immediate emergency treatment to dissolve a pre-existing clot.

questions.explanation: A patient brought to a hospital with myocardial infarction (a heart attack caused by a blocked coronary artery) is normally immediately given Streptokinase (B).Streptokinase is a type of medication known as a thrombolytic agent. Its main function is to activate plasminogen into plasmin, an enzyme that dissolves the fibrin network of a blood clot (thrombus). The immediate goal of treating a heart attack is to restore blood flow to the heart muscle as quickly as possible, and streptokinase is used to achieve this by chemically dissolving the obstructive coronary clot.

questions.text: A condition in which heart is present towards right side of the chest is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dextrocardia is a congenital condition in which the heart is located on the right side of the chest instead of the left.
Levocardia is the normal position of the heart in the left side of the chest.
A person typically has their heart on either the left or the right, not both simultaneously in this context.
This is incorrect as dextrocardia describes the condition.

questions.explanation: Dextrocardia is a congenital condition in which the heart is located on the right side of the chest instead of the left.

questions.text: The frog’s heart, when taken out of the body, continues to beat for sometime. Select the best reason for this from the following statements.(1) The frog is a poikilotherm.(2) The frog does not have any coronary circulation.(3) Its heart is “myogenic” in nature.(4) Its heart is autoexcitable.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option highlights the heart's ability to initiate a heartbeat on its own, but it doesn't account for the muscle-driven nature of the frog’s heartbeat, which is crucial for understanding why it continues to beat outside the body.
These statements are not directly related to why the frog's heart continues beating outside the body. Being poikilothermic or lacking coronary circulation doesn't explain the autonomous beating.

Correct! The frog’s heart is both myogenic, meaning it can contract on its own due to muscle tissue, and autoexcitable, meaning it can generate its own electrical impulses, which together explain its ability to continue beating outside the body.
While the myogenic nature of the frog’s heart is important, the autoexcitability is also a key factor in explaining why the heart continues to beat independently of the body.

questions.explanation: The frog’s heart is myogenic, i.e. its heartbeat originates from muscles of heart. Hence, it is autorythmic. A wave of contraction originates from the sinus venosus and spreads to the wall of sinus venosus and both auricles. This compels the heart to beat. Due to this reason, the frog’s heart will continue to beat as long as it gets a supply of ATP.

questions.text: Most of the minerals enter the root hairs of roots along with water in the form:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active transport involves the use of energy to move substances across cell membranes, usually against a concentration gradient. This is not how minerals and water typically enter root hairs in bulk.

Diffusion is the passive movement of molecules from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration. While some minerals may enter through diffusion, it does not involve the combined movement of water and minerals into root hairs.
Bulk flow refers to the movement of water and solutes together due to a pressure gradient, such as water potential differences. This is the primary way minerals enter root hairs along with water.
Facilitated diffusion involves the passive movement of molecules across membranes via specific transport proteins. It does not account for the bulk movement of water and minerals together.

questions.explanation: Most of the minerals enter the root hairs of roots and are transported up the plant along with water in the form of Bulk flow (C).While individual mineral ions are actively transported across the root hair cell membranes (the first step of uptake), once these ions enter the xylem vessels, they are carried along passively with the water. The main force driving the movement of the entire xylem sap—the water and all the dissolved minerals—is the transpiration pull (negative pressure) from the leaves, which causes the entire fluid to move together as a single unit or bulk flow. This is a much faster and more efficient method of long-distance transport than diffusion.

questions.text: Transpiration increases with increase in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Availability of light to the plant
options.explanation: While light can influence transpiration by opening stomata, it is not the primary factor that increases transpiration rate significantly.
Dry air increases the rate of water diffusion from the plant, leading to higher transpiration.
Wind can remove the humid air layer around leaves, enhancing transpiration, but it is not as impactful as the dryness of the atmosphere.
Soil water availability affects the plant's ability to maintain transpiration but does not directly increase the rate of transpiration.

questions.explanation: When air is dry, the rate of diffusion of water molecules, from the surfaces of mesophyll cells, air spaces, and through stomata to outside the leaf, increases. So more water is lost, increasing the rate of transpiration. In humid air the diffusion rate is reduced. This decreases the rate of transpiration appreciably.

questions.text: It is not directly related to the rate of transpiration:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Temperature affects the rate of transpiration because higher temperatures increase the evaporation rate of water from leaf surfaces.
Light influences transpiration by causing stomata to open, which increases water loss through transpiration.
Cellular respiration is primarily concerned with energy production within cells and does not directly influence the rate of water loss in plants, thus not affecting transpiration.
Wind increases the rate of transpiration by removing the humid air around the leaf surface, allowing more water vapor to escape.

questions.explanation: Cellular respiration is not directly related to the rate of transpiration. There are some important factors which affect the rate of transpiration in a plant.i. Light ii. Temperatureiii. CO2 concentrationiv. Humidity and vapour pressurev. Windvi. Availability of soil water

questions.text: Which plant tissue system is similar to the human circulatory system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The vascular cambium and cork cambium are secondary meristems that are formed in stems and roots after the tissues of the primary plant body have differentiated. The vascular cambium is responsible for increasing the diameter of stems and roots and for forming woody tissue.

This is correct. A plant's vascular tissues transport nutrients throughout the plant, just as the circulatory system transports nutrients throughout human bodies. While blood is the primary solvent for nutrients in humans, water is the primary solvent for nutrients in plants. Animals, however, use blood pressure to propel nutrients throughout the body while plants use gravity and the cohesive properties of water to transport nutrients.The two primary types of plant vascular tissue are xylem, which transports water, and phloem, which transports organic molecules like glucose.

This is incorrect. Dermal tissue is a thin layer of cells covering the soft parts of a plant. The dermal tissue system protects the soft tissues of plants and controls interactions with the plants' surroundings. The epidermis is a dermal tissue that is usually a single layer of cells covering the younger parts of a plant.

This is incorrect. Sclerenchyma is a specialised tissue, adapted to withstand both compressive and tensile stresses in plants. Sclerenchyma cell types may be divided into fibres, associated with phloem, xylem and other tissues; and sclereids or varied kinds.

questions.explanation: The plant tissue system that is similar to the human circulatory system is the Vascular tissue (B).The human circulatory system is responsible for the transport of essential substances like O2, nutrients, and waste products throughout the body. Similarly, the plant's vascular tissue system (composed of xylem and phloem) is the plant's main transport network. The xylem transports water and minerals from the roots up to the leaves, and the phloem transports sugars (food) from the leaves (source) to the rest of the plant (sink). Both the human circulatory system and the plant vascular system are vital for the distribution of materials necessary for life.

questions.text: Which of the following is an accurate comparison between sieve tube elements and vessel elements?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Vessel elements transport nutrients while sieve tube elements transports mostly water
options.explanation: Sieve tube elements are part of the phloem tube, so they do not function in transporting water. A better statement is present, hence this is not correct.

options.text: Vessel elements are living cells and sieve tube elements are technically dead
options.explanation: Vessel elements are more or less dead, however, the sieve tube elements contain living tissue.

options.text: Sieve tube elements are in xylem tissue and vessel elements are in phloem tissue
options.explanation: Sieve tube elements are present in phloem tissue, this is incorrect as an option.

options.text: Sieve tube elements are living cells while vessel elements are technically dead
options.explanation: Vessel elements are cells in phloem tissue that are technically dead. Upon maturation, the living material in the cells disappears while the cell wall remains; the cells are dead; however, sieve tube elements contain living tissue. This is true.

questions.explanation: The accurate comparison is that Sieve tube elements are living cells while vessel elements are technically dead (D).Both sieve tube elements and vessel elements are highly specialized for transport.Vessel Elements (Xylem): These cells die at maturity, losing all cellular contents (protoplast) and forming rigid, hollow tubes with lignified walls. This dead, hollow structure allows for the efficient bulk flow of water under tension.Sieve Tube Elements (Phloem): These cells are alive at maturity but are highly modified; they lack a nucleus, vacuole, and most other organelles to maximize space for sap flow. However, because they retain a peripheral cytoplasm and are actively maintained by adjacent companion cells (which provide ATP for loading and unloading sugars), they are fundamentally classified as living cells, unlike the dead vessel elements.

questions.text: Which of these correctly matches the plant cells to their tissue systems?I. Guard cellII. Palisade mesophyll cellIII. Sieve- tube member
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: I = dermal; II = ground; III = vascular
options.explanation: This is the correct match. Guard cells are part of the dermal tissue system, palisade mesophyll cells belong to the ground tissue system, and sieve-tube members are part of the vascular tissue system.

options.text: I = dermal; II = vascular; III = ground
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because palisade mesophyll cells, which are photosynthetic, belong to the ground tissue system, not the vascular system.

options.text: I = ground; II = dermal; III = vascular
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because guard cells are part of the dermal tissue, not ground tissue, and palisade mesophyll cells are part of the ground tissue, not dermal tissue.

options.text: I = ground; II = vascular; III = dermal
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it incorrectly classifies all three cell types: guard cells should be dermal, palisade mesophyll should be ground, and sieve-tube members should be vascular.

questions.explanation: Guard cells, which make up the stomata of the epidermis, are classified as dermal tissue. Palisade mesophyll cells, which carry out photosynthesis, are classified as ground tissue. Sieve-tube members, which make up the phloem, are classified as vascular tissue.

questions.text: Which of these increase the movement of sap within the xylem?I. Water moves into guard cells’ vacuoles.II. Humidity increases in surrounding air.III. Root endodermis prevents ions from entering xylem.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct. Water moving into guard cells increases turgor pressure, causing them to swell. This opens the stomatal pores, facilitating transpiration and drawing water up through the xylem.
Incorrect. Increasing humidity reduces transpiration by decreasing the water vapor gradient between the leaf and surrounding air, thus not enhancing the movement of sap within the xylem.
Incorrect. While the root endodermis plays a role in regulating ion entry into the xylem, preventing ions from entering would not increase sap movement.
Incorrect. Only the first statement is correct. The root endodermis preventing ions from entering the xylem would not enhance sap movement.

questions.explanation: The only process that directly leads to an increase in the movement of sap within the xylem is Water moves into guard cells’ vacuoles (I) (A).The primary mechanism for the movement of sap in the xylem is the transpiration pull (Cohesion-Tension theory). The water moves into the guard cells, making them turgid and causing the stomata to open. Open stomata increase the rate of transpiration (water evaporation from the leaves). This rapid water loss creates a strong tension (negative pressure) that pulls the continuous column of water and sap up the xylem vessels from the roots to the leaves, thus increasing the rate of movement.

questions.text: Reverse isolation, also known as protective isolation or reverse barrier nursing, is a practice used to protect individuals with weakened immune systems from potential infections. It involves creating a controlled environment through measures such as isolation rooms, controlled air flow, and the use of personal protective equipment (PPE) to prevent the entry of pathogens. This practice is commonly implemented in healthcare settings to minimise the risk of infections for vulnerable patients.This technique can be used in treatment of which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Patients with tuberculosis are typically placed in source isolation to prevent them from spreading the infection to others. Reverse isolation is not typically used for tuberculosis patients, as it is intended to protect the patient from infections, not others from the patient.

options.text: Patient who has had a minor surgery
options.explanation: Minor surgeries do not generally compromise the immune system to the extent that reverse isolation is required. Standard post-operative care is usually sufficient to prevent infections in such cases.
Glaucoma is an eye condition and does not typically affect the immune system, so reverse isolation is not necessary for these patients.

Patients with leukaemia often have weakened immune systems due to the disease itself and treatments such as chemotherapy. Reverse isolation is used to protect these patients from potential infections.

questions.explanation: Reverse isolation (or protective isolation) is a technique used to safeguard patients whose immune systems are severely weakened and cannot fight off common infections. This technique is most commonly and appropriately used in the treatment of a Patient with leukaemia (D).Leukaemia patients often undergo intensive treatments like chemotherapy that destroy rapidly dividing cells, including the healthy white blood cells (neutrophils) crucial for fighting pathogens. This state, known as neutropenia, leaves the patient highly susceptible to life-threatening infections from the environment, visitors, or staff. Reverse isolation, which uses strict hygiene, filtered air, and personal protective equipment for anyone entering the room, creates a germ-free barrier to minimize this risk.

questions.text: A patient’s chart indicates a cardiac output of 7500 mL per minute and a stroke volume of 50 mL. What is the patient’s pulse, in beats per minute?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This value is too low. It results from incorrectly dividing the cardiac output by twice the stroke volume.
This value is obtained by incorrectly assuming the cardiac output is 5000 mL instead of 7500 mL.
This is the correct pulse rate, calculated by dividing the cardiac output (7500 mL) by the stroke volume (50 mL).
This value is unrealistically high for a human pulse and results from a miscalculation.

questions.explanation: To calculate the pulse cardiac output is divided with the stroke volumeCardiac output= heart rate x stroke volume(heart rate= pulses in beats per min)7500/50 =150 heart beats per min

questions.text: The path of blood through the circulatory system is
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Right atrium → Right ventricle → Pulmonary arteries → Lungs → Pulmonary veins → Left atrium → Left ventricle → Aorta → Body
options.explanation: The blood pathway described in this option correctly follows the sequence in which blood flows beginning from the right atrium and ending at the body through the aorta.

options.text: Left atrium → Left ventricle → Pulmonary arteries → Lungs → Pulmonary veins → Right atrium → Right ventricle → Aorta → Body
options.explanation: This option incorrectly starts the sequence from the left atrium instead of the right atrium, and places the pulmonary arteries and veins in the wrong order.

options.text: Right atrium → Pulmonary veins → Right ventricle → Pulmonary arteries → Lungs → Left atrium → Left ventricle → Aorta → Body
options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that blood flows from the right atrium directly to the pulmonary veins, which is not possible as the pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium.

options.text: Right ventricle → Right atrium → Pulmonary arteries → Lungs → Pulmonary veins → Left atrium → Left ventricle → Aorta → Body
options.explanation: This option incorrectly suggests that blood flows from the right ventricle to the right atrium instead of from the right atrium to the right ventricle.

questions.explanation: Blood comes into the right atrium from the body, moves into the right ventricle and is pushed into the pulmonary arteries in the lungs. After picking up oxygen, the blood travels back to the heart through the pulmonary veins into the left atrium, to the left ventricle and out to the body's tissues through the aorta.

questions.text: If the atrioventricular (AV) pathway of the heart is blocked, the electrodes of artificial pacemaker are attached to the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The auricle is part of the atria and is not involved in the conduction pathway that requires a pacemaker intervention in case of an AV block. Stimulating the auricle would not effectively restore the heart's rhythm.

This is correct. If the AV pathway is blocked, the electrical signal from the atria cannot reach the Ventricles. Therefore, the pacemaker must be able to directly stimulate the Ventricle (usually the right Ventricle) to ensure it contracts and pumps blood. The lead is placed in the Ventricle to overcome the block.
The coronary arteries supply blood to the heart muscle and are not involved in electrical conduction. Attaching pacemaker electrodes here would not address an AV block.
The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood to the heart but do not participate in the heart's electrical conduction system. Thus, they are not suitable for pacemaker electrode placement.

questions.explanation: If the Atrioventricular (AV) pathway is blocked, the pacemaker electrodes are primarily attached to the Ventricle (B).The AV pathway (which includes the AV node and Bundle of His) is the crucial link that transmits the electrical impulse from the atria to the ventricles. When this path is blocked (e.g., in a third-degree heart block), the atria and ventricles beat independently. To maintain a functional heartbeat and proper blood circulation, the artificial pacemaker must directly stimulate the ventricles (the main pumping chambers) to contract at a regular, programmed rate, thereby bypassing the non-functional AV conduction system. This is often done using a dual-chamber pacemaker (leads in the right atrium and right ventricle) to maintain synchronization, or a single-chamber ventricular pacemaker (lead in the right ventricle) if necessary.

questions.text: A new born baby is diagnosed with a condition in which ductus arteriosus fails to close which results in blueness of his skin. The skin of the baby appears blue because of the mixing of blood between:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ductus arteriosus connects the pulmonary artery to the aorta in fetal circulation. If it remains open, it allows mixing of deoxygenated and oxygenated blood, causing cyanosis.
The left atrium and left ventricle are both part of the oxygenated blood circuit in the heart, so mixing here would not cause cyanosis.
The right atrium and right ventricle deal with deoxygenated blood, and mixing here is part of the normal flow to the lungs for oxygenation, not a cause of cyanosis.
The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs, and the pulmonary vein carries oxygenated blood back to the heart. Mixing here would not cause cyanosis.

questions.explanation: The blueness of the baby's skin (cyanosis) is caused by the mixing of blood between the Pulmonary artery and aorta (A).The Ductus arteriosus is a structure that is necessary in the fetus to bypass the lungs. After birth, it normally closes, but if it remains open (a condition called Patent Ductus Arteriosus or PDA), it creates an abnormal connection between the Aorta (which carries oxygen-rich blood) and the Pulmonary artery (which carries oxygen-poor blood to the lungs).In severe cases, particularly if the pressure in the lungs is very high (Pulmonary Hypertension), oxygen-poor blood from the Pulmonary Artery can be shunted into the Aorta, causing a portion of the blood that goes out to the body to be less oxygenated. This mixture of oxygen-rich and oxygen-poor blood leads to a lower overall oxygen saturation in the systemic circulation, causing the characteristic blue discoloration of the skin known as cyanosis.

questions.text: What will directly stimulate the sinoatrial node (SAN), causing an increase in heart rate?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: High blood carbon dioxide levels can signal the body to increase heart rate indirectly by affecting blood pH levels and stimulating respiratory centers, but they do not directly act on the sinoatrial node.

Low blood oxygen levels trigger compensatory mechanisms to increase heart rate, primarily through the sympathetic nervous system and the release of adrenaline, which indirectly affects the sinoatrial node.
Low blood pressure can lead to the release of adrenaline as the body attempts to restore normal pressure levels, indirectly affecting the sinoatrial node to increase heart rate.
Adrenaline, or epinephrine, is released in response to stress or exercise and directly stimulates the sinoatrial node, increasing the heart rate by causing more frequent electrical impulses.

questions.explanation: The sinoatrial node (SAN), also known as the natural pacemaker of the heart, is responsible for initiating the electrical impulses that regulate heart rate. The release of adrenaline, also called epinephrine, is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands in response to various stimuli, such as stress or exercise. Adrenaline acts on the SAN and increases heart rate by directly stimulating it. This stimulation leads to an increased frequency of electrical impulses generated by the SAN, resulting in a faster heart rate.

questions.text: The diagram gives information about blood pressure in various parts of the circulatory system during the cardiac cycle. At which point does the semilunar valve of the aorta close?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A represents a point where ventricular systole is beginning, and the semilunar valves are opening, not closing. Therefore, this is incorrect.
Option B indicates a point during ventricular systole when the semilunar valves are open to allow blood flow into the aorta, not when they close. Hence, this is not the correct answer.
Option C correctly identifies the point at the end of ventricular systole and the beginning of ventricular diastole, when the aortic semilunar valve closes to prevent the backflow of blood.
Option D denotes a point during diastole when the heart is filling with blood and the semilunar valves are already closed. This is not the point at which they close, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The semilunar valve of the aorta closes during the diastolic phase of the cardiac cycle. Specifically, it closes at the end of ventricular systole and the beginning of ventricular diastole. This closure of the semilunar valve prevents the backflow of blood from the aorta into the left ventricle.To provide a bit more context, the cardiac cycle consists of two main phases: diastole and systole. During diastole, the heart is in a relaxed state, and the chambers (including the ventricles) fill with blood. During systole, the heart contracts to pump the blood out of the chambers.In the specific case of the aortic semilunar valve, it opens during ventricular systole when the left ventricle contracts and pumps blood into the aorta. As the ventricular systole ends and ventricular diastole begins, the pressure in the aorta decreases. This decrease in pressure causes the aortic semilunar valve to close, preventing blood from flowing back into the left ventricle. The closure of the semilunar valve produces the characteristic "dub" sound heard during a heartbeat.

questions.text: The mammalian circulatory system is described as a closed double circulation.The diagram shows the circulatory system in a different organism. The arrows show the direction of blood flow in the vessels.​​How is the circulatory system in this organism described?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A closed double circulatory system involves two distinct loops: the pulmonary and systemic circuits. This allows for separation of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, which is characteristic of mammals, birds, and some reptiles. The system depicted does not show such separation, indicating it's not a closed double system.

A closed single circulatory system consists of a single loop where blood passes through the heart once per complete circuit of the body. This system is typical of fish and some amphibians, where blood flows from the heart to the gills for oxygenation and then directly to the rest of the body. The diagram indicates such a flow pattern.

An open double circulatory system does not exist in biology. Typically, open systems involve hemolymph flowing freely without distinct pulmonary and systemic circuits. The description of a double system in open circulation is a conceptual error.

In an open single circulatory system, blood (hemolymph) is not confined entirely to vessels and directly bathes organs. This system is found in some invertebrates like mollusks and arthropods. The diagram shows a closed system, indicating it's not an open single system.

questions.explanation: A closed single circulatory circuit is a type of circulatory system found in some animals where the blood flows through the heart and circulates through the body in a single loop. It is a simpler circulatory system compared to the closed double circulatory system found in mammals and birds. Here's how the closed single circulatory circuit works:Heart: The heart in animals with a closed single circulatory circuit consists of two chambers: an atrium and a ventricle. The atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body, while the ventricle pumps this blood out to the gills or respiratory surfaces for oxygenation. In animals with gills, the deoxygenated blood from the ventricle is pumped to the gills, where oxygen is extracted from the surrounding water and diffuses into the blood. In animals with respiratory surfaces like lungs, the deoxygenated blood is pumped to the lungs, where it picks up oxygen and releases carbon dioxide through the process of respiration. After oxygenation, the blood becomes oxygenated and is now ready to be distributed to the body tissues. From the gills or lungs, the oxygenated blood flows back to the heart to complete the circulatory loop. Examples of animals with a closed single circulatory circuit include fishes and some amphibians.

questions.text: Normal venous pressure in the feet is 3.3 kPa. When a person stands very still venous bloodpressure in the feet rises to 5.0 kPa.What causes the high pressure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Muscles in the walls of the veins contract, reducing the diameter of the veins
options.explanation: While the contraction of vein wall muscles can influence blood flow, it is not the primary reason for increased venous pressure when standing still. This option is incorrect.

options.text: Skeletal muscles in the legs are not squeezing blood upward in the veins
options.explanation: This is correct. When standing still, the lack of skeletal muscle contraction leads to less effective blood movement upward, resulting in increased venous pressure due to blood pooling.
Systolic blood pressure refers to arterial pressure during heartbeats and is not directly responsible for venous pressure changes due to inactivity. This option is incorrect.

options.text: The semilunar valves in the veins of the leg cease to function
options.explanation: The function of valves remains intact. The increased pressure is due to reduced muscle activity, not valve dysfunction. This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: When a person stands very still, the venous blood pressure in the feet rises to 5.0 kPa due to the skeletal muscles in the legs not actively contracting and squeezing blood upward in the veins. This phenomenon is known as "venous pooling" or "venous stasis."In the legs, veins have valves that prevent the backward flow of blood. When the skeletal muscles in the legs contract, they squeeze the veins, pushing the blood upward against gravity. This muscle contraction, often aided by the movement and contraction of surrounding muscles during activities like walking, plays a significant role in promoting venous return to the heart.However, when a person stands very still, the lack of skeletal muscle activity in the legs reduces the muscle pump mechanism. As a result, blood flow slows down, and blood can accumulate or pool in the veins of the feet and lower legs. This pooling of blood increases the pressure in the veins of the feet, leading to the observed rise in venous blood pressure to 5.0 kPa.

questions.text: Doctors use a stethoscope to hear the sounds produced during each cardiac cycle. The second sound is heard when:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: AV node receives a signal from SA node
options.explanation: The AV node's reception of signals from the SA node is an electrical event involved in heart rhythm regulation, not in generating heart sounds. The second heart sound is related to mechanical valve closure, not electrical activity.

The opening of the AV valves allows blood to flow from the atria to the ventricles and is associated with the first heart sound (S1), not the second. The second heart sound is related to the closure of different valves.

options.text: Ventricular walls vibrate due to gushing in of blood from atria
options.explanation: While blood flow from the atria to the ventricles can cause minor vibrations, these are not significant enough to produce the second heart sound. The second sound is specifically due to semilunar valve closure.

options.text: Semilunar valves close down after the blood flows into vessels from ventricles
options.explanation: The semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) close after the ventricles eject blood into the major arteries. This closure prevents backflow and generates the second heart sound (S2), known as the 'dub' sound.

questions.explanation: The second heart sound (S2) is produced by the closure of the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary valves) after the ventricles have ejected blood into the aorta and pulmonary artery. This prevents backflow into the ventricles. The other options involve processes that do not produce the second heart sound: the AV node's signal reception is an electrical event without an associated sound; the opening of the AV valves produces the first heart sound (S1); and ventricular wall vibrations, though occurring, are not responsible for the distinct second heart sound.

questions.text: The diagram given here is the standard ECG of a normal person. The P-­wave represents the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The beginning of systole is marked by ventricular contraction, which occurs after the QRS complex, not during the P-wave.
This is incorrect. The end of systole is represented by the end of the T-wave, not the P-wave.
Correct! The P-wave represents the electrical excitation (depolarization) of the atria, leading to their contraction.
This is incorrect. The initiation of ventricular contraction is represented by the QRS complex, not the P-wave.

questions.explanation: (C): In the given diagram, the P­wave represents the electrical excitation (or depolarisation) of the atria, which leads to the contraction of both atria. The QRS complex represents the depolarisation of the ventricles, which initiates the ventricular contraction. The contraction starts shortly after Q and marks the beginning of the systole. The T­wave represents the return of the ventricles from the excited to the normal state (repolarisation). The end of the T­wave marks the end of systole.

questions.text: Given below is the ECG of a normal human. Which one of its components is correctly interpreted below?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Complex QRS - indicates ventricular depolarization, leading to ventricular contraction
options.explanation: The QRS complex represents the depolarization of the ventricles, which is the main pumping action of the heart. This is crucial for understanding heartbeats.

options.text: Peak T -initiation of total cardiac contraction
options.explanation: The T wave signifies the repolarization of the ventricles, which is the recovery phase and not the initiation of contraction. Therefore, this interpretation is incorrect.

options.text: Peak P and peak R - represent systolic and diastolic blood pressures
options.explanation: The P wave indicates atrial depolarization, and the R wave is part of the QRS complex related to ventricular depolarization. They do not directly correlate with blood pressure readings.

options.text: Peak P - indicates the initiation left atrial contractions
options.explanation: The P wave signifies the depolarization of both atria, not just the left atrium. Thus, this explanation is not entirely accurate.

questions.explanation: (A): The QRS complex represents the depolarisation of the ventricles, which initiates the ventricular contraction. Other options are wrongly interpreted.

questions.text: If due to some injury, the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve of the human heart is partially non­functional. What will be the immediate effect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The flow of blood into the aorta will be slowed down
options.explanation: This is an indirect and later effect, as the aorta is on the left side of the heart, while the tricuspid valve is on the right side.
This is incorrect. The function of the Sinoatrial (SA) node (the pacemaker) is electrical; it is not directly affected by the mechanical function of the valve's supporting structures.

options.text: The blood will tend to flow back into the left atrium
options.explanation: This is incorrect. The tricuspid valve is between the right atrium and the right ventricle. The valve between the left atrium and left ventricle is the Bicuspid (Mitral) valve.

options.text: The flow of blood into the pulmonary artery will be reduced
options.explanation: This is correct. The Pulmonary artery receives blood from the right ventricle. If the chordae tendineae are non-functional, the tricuspid valve will not close properly during ventricular contraction, allowing blood to leak back into the right atrium (regurgitation). This reduces the volume of blood effectively pumped forward by the right ventricle into the pulmonary artery.

questions.explanation: The 'Tricuspid valve' is the valve in the heart between the right atrium and right ventricle. It consists of three cusps that channel the flow of blood from the atrium to the ventricle. When the right ventricle contracts, forcing blood into the pulmonary artery, the tricuspid valve closes the aperture to the atrium, thereby preventing any backflow of blood. The valve reopens to allow blood to flow from the atrium into the ventricle. Thus, if the tricuspid valve is partially non­-functional, the flow of blood into the pulmonary artery will be reduced.

questions.text: Which two of the following changes (i–iv) usually tend to occur in the plain dwellers when they move to high altitudes (3,500 m or more)? (i) Increase in red blood cell size (ii) Increase in red blood cell production (iii) Increased breathing rate (iv) Increase in thrombocyte count Changes occurring are
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. Moving to high altitudes usually stimulates an increase in red blood cell production to enhance oxygen transport and an increased breathing rate to take in more oxygen.
This option is incorrect. While increased breathing rate is a common adaptation, an increase in thrombocyte count is not typically associated with high altitude adaptation.
This option is incorrect. An increase in red blood cell size is not a typical response to high altitude, and an increase in thrombocyte count is also not expected.
This option is incorrect. While there is an increase in red blood cell production, an increase in red blood cell size is not a usual adaptation to high altitudes.

questions.explanation: (A): The body undergoes numerous changes at higher elevation in order to increase oxygen delivery to cells and improve efficiency of oxygen use. The early changes include increased breathing rate, increased heart rate, and fluid shifts. The later changes includes increased red blood cell production, increased 2, 3 DPG production, and increased number of capillaries.

questions.text: Fastest distribution of some injectible material/ medicine and with no risk of any kind can be achieved by injecting it into the

options.explanation: No, injecting into capillaries is not suitable for the fastest distribution. Capillaries are very small and located in tissues, primarily for the exchange of gases and nutrients, not for rapid systemic distribution.

questions.text: A drop of each of the following is placed separately on four slides. Which of them will not coagulate?

options.explanation: Serum is the liquid part of blood after the removal of blood cells and clotting factors, including fibrinogen. It does not coagulate because it lacks the proteins necessary for clot formation.

options.text: Sample from the thoracic duct of the lymphatic system

options.text: Whole blood from the pulmonary vein
options.explanation: Whole blood contains all clotting factors, including fibrinogen, which are necessary for coagulation. Thus, it can clot when placed on a slide.
Not all options will prevent coagulation; only blood serum is incapable of coagulating due to the absence of fibrinogen.

questions.explanation: Out of the given options, only blood serum will not coagulate. Blood serum lacks clotting factors due to the removal of fibrinogen during the coagulation process, making it unable to form a clot on the slide. Options b) Sample from the thoracic duct of the lymphatic system and c) Whole blood from the pulmonary vein contain clotting factors and can coagulate if placed on a slide. Therefore, the correct answer is option a) Blood Serum.

questions.text: The cardiac pacemaker in a patient fails to function normally. The doctors find that an artificial pacemaker is to be grafted in him. It is likely that it will be grafted at the site of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The atrioventricular bundle, also known as the bundle of His, is responsible for conducting impulses from the atrioventricular node to the ventricles. It is not the primary pacemaker of the heart.
The Purkinje system consists of fibers that distribute the electrical impulse through the ventricles, causing them to contract. It is not the primary pacemaker of the heart.
The sinoatrial node (SA node) is the heart's natural pacemaker, located in the right atrium. It initiates the electrical impulse that sets the rhythm for the heartbeat.
The atrioventricular node is responsible for delaying the impulse before it passes to the ventricles, not for initiating the heartbeat. It is not the primary pacemaker.

questions.explanation: (C) :SA (sinoatrial) node is a specialized bundle of neurons located in the upper part of the right atrium of the heart. SA node is the natural cardiac pacemaker from which the heartbeat originates. If this system is damaged, it may send non­coordinated impulses to the heart chambers resulting in symptoms like irregular heart rate, tiredness, dizziness, and loss of consciousness. As the pacemaker cells create this rhythmical impulse therefore an artificial pacemaker is implanted at the site of the SA node to mimic the actions of the node and conducting system and helps to regulate heartbeat.

questions.text: In veins, valves are present to check backward flow of blood flowing at

options.explanation: Atmospheric pressure does not directly affect the blood flow within veins. Valves in veins are not there to counteract atmospheric pressure.

questions.text: A 25 years old female with chronic fatigue was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia and low blood count what is the cause of her fatigue?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Reduction in amount of Fe-S centers
options.explanation: Fe-S centers are involved in electron transport, but the reduction in their amount is not the primary cause of fatigue in iron deficiency anemia.

options.text: Lowered production of water from the electron transport chain that causes dehydration
options.explanation: While water is produced in the electron transport chain, dehydration is not directly linked to fatigue caused by iron deficiency anemia.

options.text: Iron is important for electron transport chain
options.explanation: Iron is a crucial component of the electron transport chain, and its deficiency impairs energy production, leading to fatigue.

options.text: Iron is important for NADH production
options.explanation: Iron is not directly involved in NADH production. Instead, it plays a role in the electron transport chain where NADH is utilized.

questions.explanation: Iron is a vital component of hemoglobin and the electron transport chain. In iron deficiency anemia, there is insufficient iron to support optimal function of the electron transport chain, leading to decreased ATP production and causing fatigue. Option C correctly identifies the role of iron in the electron transport chain. Option A mentions Fe-S centers, which are indeed involved in electron transport but are not the primary reason for fatigue in this context. Option B incorrectly attributes fatigue to dehydration rather than energy production. Option D misattributes the role of iron to NADH production, which is not correct.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about the heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The right ventricle pumps blood to the aorta
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The right ventricle does not pump blood to the aorta. The right ventricle's primary function is to pump deoxygenated blood to the pulmonary artery. The pulmonary artery carries the deoxygenated blood to the lungs, where it picks up oxygen and undergoes oxygenation.

options.text: The left ventricle pumps blood to the entire body
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the entire body through the systemic circulation. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and contracts forcefully to propel the blood out of the heart and into the aorta. From the aorta, the oxygenated blood is distributed to the organs and tissues throughout the body.

options.text: The right atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body through the superior and inferior vena cava. The blood returning from the body is low in oxygen and high in carbon dioxide. It then passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle.

options.text: The right ventricle pumps blood to the right atrium
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The right ventricle does not pump blood to the right atrium. Instead, the right ventricle receives deoxygenated blood from the right atrium and contracts to pump it into the pulmonary artery, which carries the blood to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.explanation: The left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the entire body through the systemic circulation. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and contracts forcefully to propel the blood out of the heart and into the aorta. From the aorta, the oxygenated blood is distributed to the organs and tissues throughout the body.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about human red blood cells. EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Red blood cells have an average lifespan of approximately 120 days. After this period, they are removed from circulation and broken down by the spleen and liver.

This statement is incorrect. Leukocytes are a different type of blood cell known as white blood cells. They play a crucial role in the immune response and protection against infections. Red blood cells are called erythrocytes.

options.text: They do not have a nucleus
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Mature red blood cells do not have a nucleus. During their development, red blood cells expel their nucleus to make space for more hemoglobin, the protein responsible for carrying oxygen.

options.text: They are formed in the bone marrow
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Red blood cells are formed in the bone marrow through a process called erythropoiesis. Stem cells in the bone marrow differentiate and mature into red blood cells under the influence of a hormone called erythropoietin.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Leukocytes are a different type of blood cell known as white blood cells. They play a crucial role in the immune response and protection against infections. Red blood cells are called erythrocytes.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about human circulation EXCEPT

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygenated blood away from the heart and distribute it to the various tissues and organs of the body. Arteries have thick muscular walls that allow them to withstand the high pressure generated by the heart's contractions and to regulate blood flow.

options.text: The pacemaker of the heart is the SA node
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The sinoatrial (SA) node, located in the right atrium of the heart, is often referred to as the natural pacemaker of the heart. It initiates the electrical signals that regulate the heart's rhythm and sets the pace for the heartbeat.

This statement is correct. Leukocytes, also known as white blood cells, play a crucial role in the immune response and defense against disease. They are responsible for identifying and attacking pathogens, foreign substances, and abnormal cells in the body.

options.text: The only artery carrying deoxygenated blood is the aorta
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The aorta is an artery that carries oxygenated blood. It receives oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart and distributes it to the rest of the body. The pulmonary artery, on the other hand, is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. It transports deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The aorta is an artery that carries oxygenated blood. It receives oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart and distributes it to the rest of the body. The pulmonary artery, on the other hand, is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. It transports deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.text: All of the following are related to blood clotting EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrinogen is a plasma protein that plays a critical role in blood clotting. When there is an injury or damage to blood vessels, fibrinogen is converted into fibrin by the action of thrombin. Fibrin forms a mesh-like structure, which helps in clot formation by trapping platelets and other blood components.
Erythrocytes, also known as red blood cells, are not directly involved in the process of blood clotting. Their primary function is to carry oxygen to the body's tissues and remove carbon dioxide.

Thromboplastin, also known as tissue factor, is an enzyme that is released from damaged tissues or activated platelets during the clotting process. It initiates a series of reactions known as the coagulation cascade, leading to the formation of a blood clot.

Calcium ions are essential for the process of blood clotting. They play a crucial role in several steps of the coagulation cascade, including the activation of specific clotting factors and the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin, which is necessary for the formation of fibrin.

questions.explanation: Erythrocytes, also known as red blood cells, are not directly involved in the process of blood clotting. Their primary function is to carry oxygen to the body's tissues and remove carbon dioxide.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about the normal direction of blood flow EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The correct direction of blood flow is from the vena cava to the right atrium, not directly to the right ventricle. The vena cavae are large veins that carry deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium. From the right atrium, blood flows through the tricuspid valve to reach the right ventricle.

This statement is correct. Blood flows from the right atrium to the right ventricle through the tricuspid valve. The tricuspid valve is located between these two chambers of the heart and ensures that blood flows in the correct direction, preventing backflow.

This statement is correct. After the left ventricle contracts, blood is pumped out through the aortic semilunar valve. This valve opens to allow blood to flow from the left ventricle into the aorta, the largest artery in the body. From the aorta, oxygenated blood is distributed to the rest of the body.

This statement is correct. After blood is oxygenated in the lungs, it returns to the heart through the pulmonary veins. The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart. From the left atrium, blood then flows through the mitral valve (bicuspid valve) to enter the left ventricle.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The correct direction of blood flow is from the vena cava to the right atrium, not directly to the right ventricle. The vena cavae are large veins that carry deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium. From the right atrium, blood flows through the tricuspid valve to reach the right ventricle.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly follows the flow of blood in the human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Right ventricle → aorta → body → left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery
options.explanation: This sequence is not correct. The blood from the right ventricle should go to the pulmonary artery, not the aorta, before it is pumped to the lungs.

options.text: Right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary vein → left atrium
options.explanation: This is the correct sequence. Blood from the right ventricle is pumped into the pulmonary artery, which carries it to the lungs. In the lungs, the blood is oxygenated, then it returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins, entering the left atrium.So, it is the correct sequence as the blood flows from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery, then to the lungs, and finally back to the left atrium via the pulmonary vein.

options.text: Left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery → pulmonary vein → right atrium
options.explanation: This sequence is not correct. It starts with the left side of the heart and ends with the right side, which is the reverse of the normal blood flow.

options.text: Left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary vein → right atrium
options.explanation: This option also reverses the normal blood flow. It starts with the left side of the heart and ends with the right side.

questions.explanation: This is the correct sequence. Blood from the right ventricle is pumped into the pulmonary artery, which carries it to the lungs. In the lungs, the blood is oxygenated, then it returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins, entering the left atrium.So, it is the correct sequence as the blood flows from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery, then to the lungs, and finally back to the left atrium via the pulmonary vein.

questions.text: All of the following are true about blood EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: White blood cells are formed in the bone marrow
options.explanation: This statement is true. White blood cells, or leukocytes, are produced in the bone marrow. They are a crucial part of the body's immune system and play a role in fighting infections and providing immune defence.

options.text: Platelets are not cells but are cell fragments
options.explanation: This statement is true. Platelets, also known as thrombocytes, are not complete cells but rather small cell fragments. They are essential for blood clotting and help in the process of hemostasis.

options.text: Arteries contain valves to help in the pumping of blood
options.explanation: This statement is not true. Arteries do not contain valves. Valves are typically found in veins, where they prevent the backflow of blood and ensure that it flows in one direction, especially in areas where blood needs to move against gravity. Arteries have thicker and more muscular walls to carry blood away from the heart.

options.text: The liquid portion of the blood is called plasma
options.explanation: This statement is true. The liquid component of blood is called plasma. Plasma is a pale yellowish fluid that makes up about 55% of total blood volume and contains water, electrolytes, proteins, hormones, waste products, and various other substances.

questions.explanation: This statement is not true. Arteries do not contain valves. Valves are typically found in veins, where they prevent the backflow of blood and ensure that it flows in one direction, especially in areas where blood needs to move against gravity. Arteries have thicker and more muscular walls to carry blood away from the heart.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about blood is FALSE?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Mature red blood cells are not nucleated
options.explanation: This statement is true. Mature red blood cells, or erythrocytes, do not have a nucleus. In their maturation process, they expel their nucleus to make more space for hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying protein. This lack of a nucleus allows them to have a biconcave shape and be more efficient at carrying oxygen.

options.text: Blood platelets are involved in the clotting process
options.explanation: This statement is true. Blood platelets, or thrombocytes, are essential for blood clotting. When there is an injury or damage to blood vessels, platelets aggregate at the site to form a plug, and they release clotting factors to help form a blood clot, which stops bleeding.

options.text: The adult spleen is a site of red blood cell development
options.explanation: This statement is FALSE. In adults, the spleen is not a site of red blood cell development. Red blood cells are primarily produced in the bone marrow throughout an individual's life. The spleen's role is to filter and remove old or damaged red blood cells from circulation and to help with immune function.

options.text: White blood cells are capable of phagocytosising foreign matter
options.explanation: This statement is true. White blood cells, or leukocytes, are part of the body's immune system and can phagocytose (engulf and digest) foreign matter such as bacteria, viruses, and other microorganisms. This is an important part of their role in protecting the body against infections.

questions.explanation: This statement is FALSE. In adults, the spleen is not a site of red blood cell development. Red blood cells are primarily produced in the bone marrow throughout an individual's life. The spleen's role is to filter and remove old or damaged red blood cells from circulation and to help with immune function.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct sequence of a cardiac impulse?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: SA node → AV node → Purkinje fibres → bundle of His → ventricles
options.explanation: This sequence is incorrect because the impulse travels from the AV node to the bundle of His before reaching the Purkinje fibres.

options.text: AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles → atria
options.explanation: This sequence is incorrect because the initial impulse originates in the SA node, not the AV node, and the final destination is the ventricles, with no backflow to the atria.

options.text: SA node → atria → AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles
options.explanation: This sequence correctly shows the impulse beginning at the SA node, spreading across the atria, reaching the AV node, and then rapidly descending through the specialized ventricular pathways.

options.text: SA node → AV node → atria → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles
options.explanation: This sequence incorrectly places the contraction of the atria after the signal has already passed through the AV node. The atrial contraction happens immediately after the SA node fires.

questions.explanation: The electrical impulse that starts the heartbeat is generated by the Sinoatrial (SA) node, the heart's natural pacemaker. This impulse immediately spreads throughout the muscle of the atria, causing them to contract and push blood into the ventricles. The signal then reaches the Atrioventricular (AV) node, where it is delayed slightly to allow the atria to fully empty. From the AV node, the impulse moves quickly into the bundle of His (also called the AV bundle) and then down the interventricular septum, where it rapidly distributes through the Purkinje fibres to the muscle walls of the ventricles, causing them to contract and pump blood out of the heart.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly traces the circulatory pathway?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Superior vena cava → right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium → left ventricle → aorta
options.explanation: This option correctly traces the circulatory pathway. The pathway begins with the superior vena cava bringing deoxygenated blood from the body into the right atrium. Blood then flows into the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs via the pulmonary artery. After oxygenation in the lungs, blood returns to the heart through the pulmonary veins, entering the left atrium. It then proceeds to the left ventricle and is pumped into the aorta for systemic circulation.

options.text: Superior vena cava → left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → right atrium → right ventricle → aorta
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it suggests that deoxygenated blood from the superior vena cava enters the left atrium, which is not the case. The correct pathway involves the right atrium receiving deoxygenated blood, not the left atrium.

options.text: Aorta → right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium → left ventricle → superior vena cava
options.explanation: This pathway is incorrect. It starts with the aorta, which carries blood away from the heart, not to it. Additionally, the blood from the aorta does not return to the right atrium in this manner.

options.text: Superior vena cava → right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary veins → lungs → pulmonary artery → left atrium → left ventricle → aorta
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it incorrectly places the pulmonary veins before the lungs and the pulmonary artery, which disrupts the actual pathway of blood circulation.

questions.explanation: The correct pathway of the circulatory system is: Superior vena cava → right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium → left ventricle → aorta. This sequence properly follows the flow of blood from the body to the heart, to the lungs for oxygenation, back to the heart, and then out to the body. Option A accurately describes this pathway. Option B incorrectly suggests that blood enters the left atrium directly from the superior vena cava. Option C starts with the aorta, which carries blood away from the heart, not toward it. Option D incorrectly sequences the pulmonary veins before the lungs and the pulmonary artery.

questions.text: The sino-atrial node is the normal pacemaker of the human heart because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is located in the right atrium
options.explanation: The SA node is a small cluster of specialised cells located in the upper part of the right atrium near the opening of the superior vena cava. However, it is not the reason for which it's considered a pacemaker.

options.text: It is the only part of the conducting system that can generate impulse
options.explanation: The SA node is the only part of the cardiac conducting system that can generate an electrical impulse spontaneously. It has intrinsic automaticity, meaning it can generate electrical impulses without requiring external stimulation. This property allows the SA node to set the pace for the rest of the heart, leading to coordinated contractions. This option is correct however, option C is the most suitable.

options.text: It can generate the maximum number of action potentials in the conducting system
options.explanation: This option is the correct answer. The SA node can generate the maximum number of action potentials in the conducting system. It has a natural firing rate of around 60-100 action potentials per minute, which is faster than other parts of the conducting system. This high firing rate ensures a regular and coordinated pattern of electrical signals that drive the heart's contractions.

options.text: It produces action potentials that are more in size than the other parts of the conducting system
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. The size of action potentials generated by the SA node is not significantly different from those generated by other parts of the conducting system. The primary distinction of the SA node is its ability to generate impulses spontaneously, not the size of the action potentials it produces.

questions.explanation: The SA node can generate the maximum number of action potentials in the conducting system. It has a natural firing rate of around 60-100 action potentials per minute, which is faster than other parts of the conducting system. This high firing rate ensures a regular and coordinated pattern of electrical signals that drive the heart's contractions.

questions.text: The given figure shows a schematic plan of blood circulation in humans with labels A to D. Identify the labels along with their functions and select the correct option.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A-Pulmonary vein - takes impure blood from body parts to heart, Po2 = 60 mm Hg

options.explanation: This suggests that label B is the pulmonary artery, but the diagram shows B carrying blood away from the heart to the lower body tissues as the aorta. Additionally, the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood, which would have a much lower oxygen pressure than 90mmHg.

options.text: C-Vena Cava - takes blood from body parts to right atrium, Pco2= 45 mm Hg
options.explanation: This is correct because label C represents the vena cava, which collects used blood from body parts and returns it to the right atrium. It accurately states that the carbon dioxide pressure in this deoxygenated blood is approximately 45mmHg.

options.text: D-Dorsal aorta - takes blood from heart to body parts, Poz 95 mm Hg
options.explanation: This labels D as the dorsal aorta, but D is actually the pulmonary artery because it is shown carrying blood toward the lungs. While the aorta does have high oxygen levels, it is represented by label B in this specific circulatory plan.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is c because label C accurately identifies the vena cava, which returns carbon dioxide-rich blood from the body to the right atrium at a pressure of 45mm Hg. The other options are incorrect because they misname the blood vessels or provide wrong partial pressure values for the oxygen and carbon dioxide they carry. This schematic highlights how the right side of the heart handles deoxygenated blood while the left side manages oxygenated blood.

questions.text: Which structure returns blood poor in oxygen to the heart?
